macro_segments.title: The Physical Layout and Composition of the Sanhedrin: Semicircular Seating and Three Rows of Scholars

micro_segments.title: The semicircular shape of the Sanhedrin and the purpose of mutual visibility among judges
The court scribes: their placement and function in recording guilty and innocent verdicts
The three rows of Talmidei Chachamim: composition, numbering, and promotion mechanism
Process of adding judges from the rows when needed; maintaining equal numbers across rows

macro_segments.title: Deriving the Semicircular Shape from Song of Songs: Expounding Shorerech Aggan Hasahar

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akha bar Chanina's derivation of the Sanhedrin's shape from the verse about the navel
Aggan as a protective vessel; Hasahar as the crescent shape of the Sanhedrin
The requirement that twenty-three judges be present in the crowd for a judge to leave; connection to mixing wine

macro_segments.title: Supporting Verses on Jewish Trustworthiness: From Wheat Mounds to Hedges of Roses

micro_segments.title: Stomach as a mound of wheat: all benefit from the Sanhedrin's reasoning and legal decisions
Hedges of roses as a metaphor for the protective barrier preventing Jewish transgression
The heretic's challenge to Rabbi Kahana about a niddah being alone with her husband; the Torah's assurance
Resh Lakish's alternative source from the pomegranate verse about the lowest Jews being full of mitzvot
Rabbi Zeira's verse about smelling the fragrant clothes; trusting even those who mislead
The story of Rabbi Zeira and the thugs: demonstrating that even the lowest can repent

macro_segments.title: Movement and Advancement of Judges Through the Rows: The Principle of Being a Lion's Tail

micro_segments.title: When judges move up, all others advance to fill vacancies; Abaye's ruling that no empty spaces are created
The objection of a judge moving from the head of the second row to the tail; the principle of being a tail to lions rather than a head to foxes

macro_segments.title: Threatening Witnesses in Capital Cases: The Magnitude and Irrevocability of Human Life

micro_segments.title: How witnesses are brought in and warned about the importance of their testimony in capital cases
Warning against testimony based on assumption, hearsay, or secondhand reports; the need for direct observation
The distinction between civil and capital cases: money can be repaid, but life is eternal
The blood of Cain's victim crying out; bloods in plural referring to his blood and all descendants
Why man was created as a single individual: to teach the infinite value of one human life
The principle that destroying one soul equals destroying a world; sustaining one soul equals sustaining a world
Additional reasons for creating one first human: preventing arrogance, preventing heretical ideas about multiple gods, demonstrating divine greatness

macro_segments.title: Threatening Witnesses in Capital Cases: The Magnitude and Irrevocability of Human Life (continued)

micro_segments.title: The obligation of each individual to say 'the world was created for my sake'

macro_segments.title: Witness Concerns and Reassurance: Addressing Fear of Responsibility for a Guilty Verdict

micro_segments.title: The witness's fear of involvement in the death of the accused and the obligation to testify
The verse requiring a witness to speak their testimony; reassurance through the verse about rejoicing in the destruction of the wicked

macro_segments.title: Circumstantial Evidence and Direct Testimony: What Constitutes Valid Witness Observation

micro_segments.title: The definition and example of assumption or circumstantial evidence: seeing one person chase another into a ruin
Finding a sword, dripping blood, and a dying victim does not constitute valid testimony of who committed the act
Rabbi Shimon ben Shatach's case: confronting the killer directly and recognizing the need for two witnesses
The verse that God knows thoughts and will punish the murderer; divine punishment by snake bite in this case

macro_segments.title: Capital Punishment After the Temple's Destruction: God's Judgment Replacing Human Courts

micro_segments.title: The teaching that even after the Sanhedrin ceased to function, the four death penalties are still carried out by divine means
The apparent contradiction about the Sanhedrin's cessation forty years before the Temple's destruction
Explanation that the Sanhedrin sometimes reconvened for special cases in the Chamber of Hewn Stone
Enumeration of how each death penalty type is carried out by natural means: stoning, burning, decapitation, strangulation
The case of the murderer killed by snake: the principle that if someone deserves multiple death penalties, the more severe is applied by Heaven

macro_segments.title: Assumption and Circumstantial Evidence in Civil vs. Capital Cases: Talmudic Debate on Standards

micro_segments.title: The distinction that in capital cases assumption is not used, implying it is used in civil cases
Rabbi Akha's example of identifying a dangerous camel as the killer of another camel through circumstantial evidence
Challenge: if assumption works in civil cases for circumstantial evidence, should it also work for secondhand testimony?
Response: secondhand testimony is invalid in both civil and capital cases; assumption differs in applicability

macro_segments.title: Interpreting 'Bloods' in the Plural: The Multiple Meanings and Implications of Kayin's Sin

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda's interpretation that Kayin struck Hevel multiple times, not knowing where the soul departs
Alternative explanation: bloods refer to Hevel's blood and the blood of all his potential descendants
The earth never again opened its mouth to receive blood after accepting Hevel's blood; distinction from Korach's case

macro_segments.title: Exile as Atonement: How Expulsion from the Land Atones for Certain Sins

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: exile atones for half of the sin, as seen in Cain's transition from perpetual wandering to settling in Nod
Rav Yehuda: exile atones for three things—sword, starvation, and pestilence—saving one's life among the besiegers
Rabbi Yochanan: exile atones for all sins, as demonstrated through the case of Yechonya who was cursed but had children after exile
Yechonya's son Shealtiel was born in jail, not in the normal way, hence his unusual name
Zerubbabel: Shealtiel's son who was seeded in Babylon and led the return from exile
Zerubbabel is also known as Nehemia ben Chachalya; discussion of the names and identities

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yehuda's opinion on three scribes
Biblical derivation from Song of Songs
Shorerech as navel of the world
Principle of tail to lions vs. head to foxes
Destroying one soul equals destroying a world
Personal obligation: world created for my sake
Natural means of death as punishment
Multiple death penalties and severity hierarchy
Evidence standards in different case types
